title: American buttermilk biscuits
subtitle: Southern flaky biscuits with cold butter
excerpt: American buttermilk biscuits with cold butter folded into the flour. The folds give the characteristic layered, flaky split; the buttermilk gives the soft tang. Twelve biscuits for breakfast or supper, ready in under thirty minutes.
sourceNotes: Fannie Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896), Project Gutenberg: documents the American baking-powder biscuit with buttermilk as the canonical Southern household form. Mary Lincoln, Mrs. Lincoln's Boston Cook Book (1884), Project Gutenberg: the rubbing-in method for shortened American biscuits is laid out in the breakfast-breads chapter. USDA Home Economics bulletins (1920s, public domain): document the fold-and-pat technique for tall biscuit layers.

content.text: American biscuits are not British biscuits; they are closer to a British scone with a Southern accent. Cold butter is rubbed into self-raising flour with buttermilk for liquid, the dough is folded once or twice to build layers, and the result is a tall biscuit that splits open in flakes. Served at breakfast with butter, jam, sausage gravy, or country ham.

The trick to a tall biscuit is three things. Use very cold butter, so small flakes are still visible in the dough when it goes in the oven. Handle the dough briefly. Bake hot, so the rise sets fast before the butter melts out.

Heat the oven to 225°C fan. Whisk the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{caster-sugar}} in a wide bowl. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} and rub it in with cold fingertips, leaving plenty of pea-sized pieces still visible. Stop before the butter disappears into the flour; you want flakes, not paste.

Pour the {{buttermilk}} in all at once. Stir with a fork until the dough comes together; it will look shaggy. Tip onto a floured surface and pat (do not roll) to a rectangle about 2.5 cm thick. Fold the dough into thirds like a letter, turn ninety degrees, and pat to a 2.5 cm slab. Fold again, turn, and pat once more. Two folds is enough; more risks toughening the dough.

Cut twelve rounds with a floured 6 cm cutter pressed straight down. Set on a lined baking tray with the biscuits touching at the sides. They will rise into each other and support each other up. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes; the tops should be deep gold and the sides should show clear layered flakes. Cool 3 minutes on the tray, then transfer to a rack. Best eaten warm.
